ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic chili sauce class (FOODON_03301710); no specific belacan chilli sauce entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers chilli sauces and pastes broadly; no specific sweet spicy belacan variant exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for belacan chilli sauce or sambal condiment found in published SNOMED CT release.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical condiment serving of sweet spicy belacan chilli sauce is approximately 1 tablespoon (15 g), used as a dipping sauce or accompaniment alongside toast, noodles, or rice dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Chilli sauce, sweet (sambal manis) — nutrient composition estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Hot sauce (NDB 02046) and Sweet chilli sauce components, cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD sambal belacan data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for sweet spicy belacan chilli sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy hot sauce data (NDB 02046) with typical sweet chilli sauce composition from IMR Malaysia MyFCD sambal belacan entries, adjusted for the sugar content characteristic of sweet variants. Energy calculated from macros using Atwater factors. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweet chilli sauce estimated from analogous sweet condiment data: table sugar (GI 65) and chilli sauce components; reference Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sweet spicy belacan chilli sauce. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the dominant sugar content (18 g/100 g sucrose-equivalent) and the diluting effect of chilli, garlic, and fibre components. Reference: Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL, Diabetes Care. Glycemic load per 15 g serving = (55 × 3.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 2.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fermented shrimp paste (belacan) and added salt
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit sweet spicy belacan chilli sauce to no more than 1 teaspoon per serving, as a single tablespoon (15 g) contributes approximately 123 mg sodium, and cumulative condiment use can significantly exceed the WHO daily sodium target of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar content (18 g/100 g) contributing to postprandial glycaemic excursion
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should use sweet spicy belacan chilli sauce sparingly; a 15 g serving contributes approximately 2.7 g free sugars and has an estimated glycaemic load of 2, which is low per serving but can accumulate with larger portions or multiple condiment servings at a meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate free sugar content with low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the primary concern with sweet spicy belacan chilli sauce is its high sodium content (820 mg/100 g), which may contribute to blood pressure elevation; saturated fat is low (0.4 g/100 g), and the capsaicin from chilli may have modest vasodilatory benefits, but portion control remains essential.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet Spicy Belacan Chilli Sauce is a Singaporean condiment made from red chillies, belacan (fermented shrimp paste), sugar, lime juice, garlic, and shallots. It is typically blended and lightly cooked to produce a glossy, thick sauce with a characteristic balance of sweetness, heat, and the pungent umami depth of fermented shrimp paste. The texture is smooth to slightly chunky, and the colour is a vivid red-orange. It is commonly served as a dipping sauce alongside kaya toast, soft-boiled eggs, noodle dishes, and grilled seafood in Singaporean coffee shops and toast chains such as Toast Box. Nutritionally, it is moderate in calories (approximately 120 kcal/100 g) with a high sugar content (18 g/100 g) and a notably high sodium level (820 mg/100 g) due to the belacan component. The sauce contains crustacean allergens from the shrimp paste and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease, while the free sugar content warrants portion awareness for those with Type 2 Diabetes. The glycaemic index is estimated at 55 (low), and the glycaemic load per standard 15 g serving is low at approximately 2.0. Capsaicin from the chilli component may offer modest anti-inflammatory and metabolic benefits at typical condiment serving sizes.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for sweet spicy belacan chilli sauce was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy hot sauce data with typical sweet chilli sauce composition from IMR Malaysia MyFCD sambal belacan entries, adjusted for sugar content. GI is an analogous estimate based on sugar content and published GI tables. Confidence is medium.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated; no exact database entry exists for this specific product. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1 as these micronutrients are not reported in the reference sources used for this estimated composition. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as this is a plant-based and seafood-paste condiment with no hydrogenated fat components. GI is an analogous estimate at 55 (low) based on sugar content and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of GI and GL. Glycaemic load per 15 g serving calculated as (55 × 3.6) / 100 ≈ 2.0. Belacan contains crustacean allergens; this sauce is not suitable for individuals with shellfish allergies. The food is classified as Singaporean cuisine for multilingual naming purposes, as belacan chilli sauce is a canonical Singaporean and Malaysian condiment served in Singaporean toast chain restaurants. Sambal Belacan Sweet added to indonesian aliases as belacan is also a recognised Indonesian ingredient term.
